text:
While political parties are often treated as unitary actors in spatial models, they are internally complex organizations driven by diverse member preferences. This article investigates the causes and consequences of intraparty preference heterogeneity by examining how electoral conditions and governing status influence internal dissent and leadership selection. We theorize that electoral losses and perceived incompetence trigger increased disagreement within a party, as rival factions challenge the dominant leadership's direction. However, we argue that the governing context acts as a stabilizer; party members grant leaders greater ideological flexibility when in power, whereas opposition parties face heightened internal pressure for policy purity—particularly when a strong economy makes the incumbent government appear more competent.

To test these propositions, we present a new dataset of intraparty actor preferences derived from automated text analysis (WORDFISH) of speeches delivered at national party congresses in Germany (CDU, SPD) and France (PS, UMP). The empirical results demonstrate that internal disagreement increases in opposition parties during periods of economic growth, reflecting a perceived failure of the leadership. Furthermore, our analysis of leadership elections reveals that while candidates closer to the party's ideological median generally receive more votes, being in government insulates candidates from the electoral penalties of deviating from that median. By opening the "black box" of intraparty politics through natural language processing, this study highlights the complex interplay between executive responsibility, economic performance, and the internal democratic life of political parties.
